text: {"claims":[{"statement":"Eggs are often categorised with other animal proteins such as beef, lamb, poultry and dairy when reporting on the environmental impact of foods, but the environmental impact of egg production is considerably lower than these alternatives.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Eggs are often categorised with other animal proteins such as beef, lamb, poultry and dairy when reporting on the environmental impact of foods, but the environmental impact of egg production is considerably lower than these alternatives.","entailment":"The quoted sentence directly states that although eggs are grouped with other animal proteins, their environmental impact is much lower, supporting the claim.","population":"","intervention":"egg production","comparator":"production of other animal proteins such as beef, lamb, poultry, dairy","outcome":"environmental impact (e.g., greenhouse gas emissions, land use)","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"The 2011 report by the World Wildlife Fund demonstrated that animal proteins are responsible for 57% of greenhouse gas emissions (GHGEs) but, of this, eggs were responsible for only 1.9% of GHGEs.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"The 2011 report by the World Wildlife Fund demonstrated that animal proteins are responsible for 57% of greenhouse gas emissions (GHGEs) but, of this, eggs were responsible for only 1.9% of GHGEs","entailment":"The sentence provides specific percentages from the WWF report, showing the proportion of GHGEs attributable to eggs versus all animal proteins.","population":"","intervention":"egg production","comparator":"other animal protein production","outcome":"share of greenhouse gas emissions","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"57% overall, 1.9% for eggs","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Producing 100 g of protein from eggs creates 3.8 kg of CO₂ equivalent on average, compared with 25 kg for beef.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Data from the English National Food Strategy revealed that producing 100 g of protein from eggs creates 3.8 kg of CO2 equivalent on average, compared with 25 kg for beef","entailment":"The quoted data directly compares CO₂ emissions per 100 g protein for eggs versus beef, supporting the claim.","population":"","intervention":"egg protein production","comparator":"beef protein production","outcome":"CO₂ equivalent per 100 g protein","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"3.8 kg vs 25 kg","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Food allergies affect around 3–8% of children and 1–3% of adults.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Food allergies affect around 3–8% of children and 1–3% of adults.","entailment":"The sentence provides prevalence percentages for food allergies in children and adults, directly supporting the claim.","population":"","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"prevalence of food allergies","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"3–8% of children, 1–3% of adults","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Egg allergy, as with most other food allergies in children, tends to be outgrown before adulthood.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Egg allergy, as with most other food allergies in children, tends to be outgrown before adulthood","entailment":"The quoted statement asserts that egg allergy is typically resolved before adulthood, supporting the claim.","population":"children with egg allergy","intervention":"","comparator":"","outcome":"resolution of allergy before adulthood","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"descriptive"},{"statement":"Early advice to delay introduction of potentially allergenic foods during weaning has been found to be counterproductive, and the current view is that such foods should be introduced when weaning commences at around 6 months.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Early advice given to parents was to delay the introduction of potentially allergenic foods (including wheat, peanuts, cow’s milk and eggs) during weaning. However, this has now been found to be counterproductive, and it is the current view that potentially allergenic foods should be introduced when weaning commences at around 6 months","entailment":"The passage describes a shift from delaying to recommending introduction at about 6 months, indicating the earlier advice is now considered counterproductive.","population":"infants being weaned","intervention":"delayed introduction of allergenic foods","comparator":"introduction at around 6 months","outcome":"effectiveness of allergy prevention","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Early introduction of potentially allergenic foods may reduce the risk of allergy in babies and children.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Indeed, contrary to previous beliefs, there is growing evidence that early introduction of potentially allergenic foods may reduce the risk of allergy in babies and children","entailment":"The sentence cites emerging evidence that early exposure is associated with a lower allergy risk, supporting the claim.","population":"babies and children","intervention":"early introduction of potentially allergenic foods","comparator":"later or delayed introduction","outcome":"risk of allergy","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"Guidance released in 2018 stated that eggs need not be differentiated from other solid foods during weaning and can be introduced from 6 months.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"In 2018, a joint paper from the UK Scientific Advisory Committee on Nutrition and the Committee on Toxicity of Chemicals in Food, Consumer Products and the Environment (COT) was released with guidance that eggs need not be differentiated from other solid foods during weaning and can be introduced from 6 months","entailment":"The quoted guidance explicitly permits introducing eggs at 6 months, supporting the claim.","population":"infants being weaned","intervention":"introduction of eggs at 6 months","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"normative"},{"statement":"For babies at higher risk of food allergy, the guidance is to introduce cooked eggs into the diet from around 4 months of age.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"The guidance for babies at higher risk of food allergy (i.e., those with eczema or other known food allergies) is to introduce cooked eggs into the diet from around 4 months of age","entailment":"The sentence provides specific guidance for high‑risk infants to start eating cooked eggs at about 4 months, supporting the claim.","population":"babies at higher risk of food allergy","intervention":"introduction of cooked eggs at ~4 months","comparator":"","outcome":"","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"around 4 months of age","modality":"normative"}]}
